'''Terry Newton''' (7 November 1978 – 26 September 2010) was an English professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a [[hooker (rugby league)|hooker]]. He began his professional career with [[Leeds Rhinos]] in 1996,<ref name="Biography at therhinos.co.uk">{{cite web|url=https://www.therhinos.co.uk/player?PlayGuid=TN279922|title=Biography at therhinos.co.uk|publisher=therhinos.co.uk|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref> before joining his hometown club [[Wigan Warriors]] in 2000.<ref name="Heritage Numbers - In Debut Order">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/readarticle.php?article_id=1822|title=Heritage Numbers - In Debut Order|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018|archive-date=27 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190327102545/http://wigan.rlfans.com/readarticle.php?article_id=1822|url-status=dead}}</ref> He later also played for [[Bradford Bulls]] and [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]]. During his career, he won two [[Challenge Cup]] medals ([[1999 Challenge Cup|1999]] with Leeds, and [[2002 Challenge Cup|2002]] with Wigan) and was a runner-up in four [[Super League Grand Final]]s. He was one of a handful of players to feature in each of the first 15 seasons of the [[Super League]].<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> At international level, he was capped 15 times by [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] between 1998 and 2007, and also represented [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Lancashire rugby league team|Lancashire]].

In February 2010, he was given a two-year ban after being one of the first sportsmen to have tested positive for human growth hormone. He died by suicide seven months later, aged 31.

==Playing career==
===Leeds Rhinos===
Newton joined the [[Leeds Rhinos]] after a protracted transfer from amateur team Wigan St Judes, after [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] had claimed they had also signed the young Newton. Terry missed his first year of Academy rugby due to the problem however when he finally joined in March 1996 he made an immediate impact, making his début for the Leeds Rhinos against the [[Sheffield Eagles]] at Headingley in April 1996.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Injured Hayes misses Wembley |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/injured-hayes-misses-wembley-1305873.html |website=The Independent |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=19 April 1996}}</ref> By 1997's [[Super League II]], he was a regular in the team making 26 appearances in 1997 and scoring 2-tries. His progression continued in 1998, despite going on loan to [[Bramley R.L.F.C.|Bramley]] for a month, he still played in the Grand Final team at [[Old Trafford]] before playing arguably his best season at the club in 1999. The Leeds Rhinos had signed Great Britain {{rlp|hk}} [[Lee Jackson (rugby league)|Lee Jackson]], and Newton's opportunities looked limited, however coach [[Graham Murray]] came up with a game plan that used both to their maximum potential. Newton, with his no nonsense direct play would start games with Jackson coming on off the bench to carve teams open. He played for Leeds Rhinos at {{rlp|hk}} in their [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by [[Wigan Warriors]].

===Wigan Warriors===
Newton joined the [[Wigan Warriors]] for a transfer fee of £145,000,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hadfield |first1=Dave |title=Terry Newton: Rugby league hooker who took his own life following his suspension for drug-taking |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/obituaries/terry-newton-rugby-league-hooker-who-took-his-own-life-following-his-suspension-for-drugtaking-2092911.html |website=The Independent |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=29 September 2010}}</ref> and he made his début on [[Boxing Day]] 1999 against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. He was a prolific try scorer for the Wigan Warriors, scoring over a dozen tries in each of his first two seasons. Newton played for the Wigan Warriors at {{rlp|hk}} in their [[2000 Super League Grand Final]] loss against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. He played for England in 2001 against Wales. He also played for the Wigan Warriors at {{rlp|hk}} in their [[2001 Super League Grand Final]] loss to the [[Bradford Bulls]]. Terry would surely have represented Great Britain in the 2001 Ashes series had it not been for a broken arm suffered in the Wigan Grand Final defeat by Bradford Bulls at Old Trafford. Newton signed a 4-year extension to his Wigan Warriors contract during August 2002. He played much of the 2003's [[Super League VIII]] despite needing a knee operation. He decided to put the operation back to the end of the year because the club was suffering an injury crisis at the time. He was rewarded for his efforts when he was included in the 2003 Super League 'Dream Team'. Newton played for the Wigan Warriors at {{rlp|hk}} in the [[2003 Super League Grand Final]] which was lost to the Bradford Bulls.

In September 2005, Newton was suspended for 10 games for two separate incidents in a match against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]],<ref>{{cite web |title=Newton handed 10-match suspension |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/4240956.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=30 October 2024 |date=13 September 2005}}</ref> which would end up being his final game for Wigan. During his time at the club, Newton featured in three [[Super League Grand Final]]s, but was on the losing side on each occasion.

===Bradford Bulls===
Newton moved to the Bradford Bulls for the start of the 2006 season as part of a swap deal involving [[Mickey Higham]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Newton to Bulls, Higham to Wigan |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/4359908.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=20 October 2005}}</ref> He was a consistent performer for the Bradford Bulls, but the club did not feature in a major final during his time there. He was allowed to leave the club following the expiry of his contract at the end of the 2009 season. Newton signed for [[Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] following the expiry of his contract at the Bradford Bulls.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wakefield/8141687.stm Wildcats close on Newton signing]</ref>

===Wakefield Trinity Wildcats===
In February 2010, Newton received a two-year ban from playing rugby league for failing a drugs test, having tested positive for [[human growth hormone]] in November the previous year.<ref>{{cite web |title=Newton banned after opting not to contest drug charge |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wakefield/8524902.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=19 February 2010}}</ref> Wakefield cancelled Newton's contract a few days later, having only played two games for the club.<ref>{{cite web |title=Wakefield cancel Terry Newton's contract after drug ban |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wakefield/8528445.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=22 February 2010}}</ref>

===International===
Newton gained two caps in the 1998 Great Britain v New Zealand test series whilst at Leeds Rhinos. Newton won international honours with two caps for England against France in October 1999. He played 83 times for Leeds Rhinos and scored 6-tries.

Newton won [[Cap (sport)|cap]]s for Great Britain while at the Wigan Warriors in 2002 against Australia (sub), in 2003 against Australia (3 matches), in 2004 against Australia (3 matches), and New Zealand.

He made his return to the Great Britain squad for the 2003 Ashes series, despite needing a knee operation. Newton was selected in the [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain team]] to compete in the end of season [[2004 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] he played at {{rlp|hk}} in the Lions' 44–4 loss.

While at the Bradford Bulls he played for Great Britain in 2006 against New Zealand (2 matches), New Zealand (sub), and Australia (2 matches), and in 2007 against New Zealand.

==Personal life==
Newton was married to his wife, Stacey, and had two daughters, Millie and Charley-Mia.<ref>{{cite web |title=Terry Newton obituary |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/sep/27/terry-newton-obituary |website=The Guardian |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=27 September 2010}}</ref>

Following his ban from rugby league, Newton became a [[public house|pub licensee]].<ref name=BBC12041923/> His autobiography, ''Coming Clean'', was released in June 2010.<ref>{{cite web |title=Friday Interview - Terry Newton: Newton will not blow whistle as he decides to come clean |url=https://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/friday-interview-terry-newton-newton-will-not-blow-whistle-as-he-decides-to-come-clean-1971411 |website=Yorkshire Post |access-date=29 October 2024 |date=18 June 2010}}</ref>

===Death===
On 26 September 2010, Newton was found hanged in a house on Harswell Close<ref name="Guardian">{{cite news|title=Former Great Britain hooker Terry Newton found dead|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/sep/26/great-britain-terry-newton-dead|access-date=26 September 2010|newspaper= [[The Guardian]]|date=26 September 2010}}</ref> in the [[Orrell, Greater Manchester|Orrell]] district of [[Metropolitan Borough of Wigan|Wigan]].<ref name="BBC" /> A later post mortem found traces of [[cocaine]], [[amphetamine]]s and the [[steroid]] [[nandrolone]] within his system, prescription [[anti-depressant]]s, and consumption of alcohol, all of which could have impaired his judgement. Newton had left several notes stating he wanted to die. Coroner Jennifer Leeming recorded an open verdict.<ref name=BBC12041923>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-12041923|title=Hanged rugby league player Terry Newton had taken drugs|publisher=BBC News|date=20 December 2010|access-date=21 December 2010}}</ref>
